source_text:
The Italian universal genius Leonardo da Vinci not only painted the Mona Lisa and other beautiful pictures, he was also a brilliant engineer. Many of his sketches and descriptions of his inventions have survived to this day. Gears played a significant role in his inventions. The gear itself already existed, but Da Vinci developed numerous machines from it. For example, the chain drive mechanism, which we find today in every bicycle.
Or a heavy-duty forklift. Slightly modified, this corresponds to today's car lift.
He developed many weapons on behalf of his employers.
For example, this organ gun. Quasi the first machine gun.
And even a tank that, however, due to a construction defect could not move forward.
Bitte übersetzen Sie den Text aus dem Deutschen ins Englische.
Subtitling for ZDF for funk, 2017. Quasi the first machine gun. And even a tank that couldn't move forward due to a design flaw. Da Vinci is also considered the father of bionics. For his drawings of flying machines, he observed how birds move their wings. Afterward, he designed a flying device with wings that humans should be able to move on their own. An ornithopter. But it remained purely imaginary; no human would be strong enough to take off with it. With the propeller, four people were supposed to rotate vertically into the air and fly—a precursor to the helicopter, but this device was too heavy; there were still no lightweight materials like plastic or aluminum. Despite this, Da Vinci did not abandon the dream of flying.
His design of a parachute. A success, albeit only after 500 years.
The British Adrian Nicholas sailed through the air in 2000.
summary: Leonardo da Vinci, the Italian universal genius, was not only a renowned painter known for works like the Mona Lisa but also an innovative engineer. His inventions included the development of various machines using gears, such as the chain drive mechanism found in modern bicycles and forklifts. He also created advanced weaponry and a tank with a design flaw preventing movement. Da Vinci is also recognized as a pioneer in bionics, inspired by bird flight mechanics to design an ornithopter, though it remained purely theoretical due to insufficient human strength. He envisioned a propeller-driven device for vertical flight, a concept precursor to the helicopter, but deemed too heavy for practical use. Despite these challenges, Da Vinci's dream of flight persisted, leading to the creation of a successful parachute, which was only achieved 500 years later by Adrian Nicholas in 2000.
